message:
feat(security): implement proof-carrying taint reduction via TaintReduction [MOR/C3.2]

Delete bare TaintFlags::remove() and replace with reduce_with_proof()
which requires a TaintReduction proof struct referencing a SanitizerReceipt.
ProvenanceRecord::apply_taint_reduction() now constructs the proof before
clearing flags, maintaining the audit trail. 8 new acceptance tests.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

-{"id":"flywheel_connectors-1qmep","title":"[MOR/C3.6] Add sealed trait pattern to FcpConnector and archetype traits","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-08T03:34:58.511638Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-08T03:37:08.245348Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["connector","sdk","type-safety"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-1qmep","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-8whb3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-08T03:37:08.244638Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2645,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-1qmep","author":"jemanuel","text":"WHAT: Add sealed trait pattern to FcpConnector and all archetype traits (RequestResponse, Streaming, Bidirectional, Polling, Webhook). This prevents external crates from implementing these traits, ensuring only connectors that go through the official SDK pathway can implement the connector contract. WHY: A7 Type-Theoretic found that external crates can implement FcpConnector or archetype traits, potentially breaking security invariants. The sealed trait pattern ensures the trait hierarchy is closed — only types within the FCP workspace can implement it. This prevents a malicious or buggy external crate from claiming to be an FcpConnector without meeting the security requirements. IMPLEMENTATION: ```rust // In fcp-sdk or fcp-core: mod private { pub trait Sealed {} } pub trait FcpConnector: private::Sealed + Send + Sync { ... } // Implement Sealed for the BaseConnector and any approved types: impl private::Sealed for BaseConnector {} // Proc macro: #[derive(FcpConnector)] automatically implements Sealed ``` WHERE: crates/fcp-sdk/src/lib.rs or crates/fcp-core/src/connector.rs — the trait definitions. If connectors define their own types, each connector type must impl Sealed (can be done via derive macro or blanket impl for types that impl a marker). ACCEPTANCE: (1) private::Sealed trait exists. (2) FcpConnector requires Sealed. (3) All archetype traits require Sealed. (4) BaseConnector implements Sealed. (5) External crates cannot implement FcpConnector (compile error). (6) Existing 150 connectors still compile (they go through fcp-sdk which provides Sealed). (7) Doc test showing external impl fails. ESTIMATED EFFORT: 1-2 days. LOWER PRIORITY because the current risk is theoretical (no known external implementations), but this closes the hole permanently.","created_at":"2026-04-08T03:36:51Z"}]}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1r09","title":"[POL] CLI workflow (bundle/diff/preview/apply/rollback)","description":"# [POL] CLI workflow (bundle/diff/preview/apply/rollback)

## Goal
Expose policy bundle workflows via CLI for operators and CI.

## Commands
- `fcp policy bundle create --zone <id> --from <current> --sign`
- `fcp policy bundle diff <old> <new>`
- `fcp policy preview --bundle <id> --samples <file>`
- `fcp policy apply <bundle>` / `fcp policy rollback --to <bundle>`

## Output Requirements
- JSON and human outputs; stable ordering.
- Non-zero exit codes on validation or preview failures.

## Tests
- Unit tests for CLI parsing + output formatting (snapshots).
- Integration tests with test bundles and preview fixtures.
- Structured JSON logs for each stage (create/diff/preview/apply/rollback).

## Acceptance Criteria
- CLI outputs are deterministic and actionable.
- Rollback is one command and emits an audit event.","notes":"Follow-up: CLI bundle create now uses POLICY_BUNDLE_SIGNED_FIELDS for signed_fields ordering.

[AlienArtifactPractical]
- Add evidence ledger fields with per-check log-odds contributions and explicit loss decision.
- Add anytime-valid risk control (e-process) for approvals/denials and record epsilon in receipts.
[ExtremeOptimizationPractical]
- Compile policies to DAG/BDD and cache allow/deny results with LRU.
- Use bitset capability sets for fast subset checks.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-4zr0","title":"[IDEA-21] Zone Policy Diff + Rollback Tooling","description":"## Background
Zone policies change over time. Operators need a safe way to review diffs and roll back with clear evidence and approvals.

## Goals
- Compute diffs between ZonePolicy/ZoneDefinition objects.
- Generate rollback plans and highlight risky changes.
- Expose CLI output for audits and CI.

## Non-goals
- Automatic policy edits without approval.

## Approach
- Implement a structured diff engine for zone policy objects.
- Add CLI `fcp policy diff` and `fcp policy rollback --plan`.
- Require ApprovalToken for rollbacks in high-trust zones.

## Risks
- Diff noise from ordering changes; enforce canonical ordering before diff.

## Success Criteria
- Operators can see precise changes to capabilities, transport policy, and ceilings.
- Rollback outputs are deterministic and auditable.

## Acceptance Criteria (Refined)
- Diff flags risk‑increasing changes (capabilities, transport policy).
- Rollback plan is deterministic and auditable.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-503","title":"fcp.terraform: Drift detection between state and actual resources","description":"# fcp.terraform: Drift detection between state and actual resources (FCP2)

## Goal
Detect drift safely by comparing:
- recorded Terraform state
- actual provider-reported infrastructure

This may require provider API egress and must respect NetworkConstraints.

## Scope
- Run a refresh-only plan (no apply) and extract drift summary.
- Return a canonical `DriftReport`:
  - resources changed
  - attributes changed (hashed/summary)
  - severity estimation

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `terraform.drift.read` (sensitive)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints default deny unless backend/providers are explicitly allowed.
- No mutations; drift detection must not apply changes.
- Audit state/provider access.

## Deliverables
- Operation schema:
  - `terraform.drift.detect`
- Canonical output type:
  - `DriftReport`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Unit tests with a terraform shim for:
  - refresh-only invocation
  - parsing drift summaries
  - error mapping

## Acceptance criteria
- Drift reports are deterministic and safe-by-default.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-51o","title":"fcp.airtable: Linked record resolution and management","description":"# fcp.airtable: Linked Record Resolution and Management

## Goal
Provide first-class support for Airtable relational features:
- linked record fields
- lookup/rollup-style derived values

## External surface (NetworkConstraints)
Default deny.
- Network egress MUST be restricted per-operation via manifest `NetworkConstraints`.
- Allow only the configured service host(s); HTTPS-only where applicable; deny redirects by default.
- Bound timeouts + response sizes; treat all remote responses as untrusted input; never leak secrets/PII in logs or errors.

## Zone model (mechanical)

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

Recommended deployment pattern: run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies.

## Scope
- Read/write “link to another record” fields (arrays of record ids).
- Resolve linked record chains for agent-friendly outputs (bounded joins).
- Support computed field read semantics (lookup/rollup) without pretending they are writable.

## Key requirements
- Capability gated (reads vs writes).
- Bounded traversal:
  - prevent unbounded graph walks / cycles
  - explicit depth/record count limits
- Deterministic output:
  - stable ordering when expanding linked records
  - explicit “partial” indicators when traversal is truncated by bounds

## Tests
- Linked graph traversal (including cycles).
- Bounded expansion behavior.
- Error mapping for missing/deleted linked records.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Linked record expansion is safe and predictable.
- Computed fields are exposed read-only with clear typing.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-556f","title":"TEST: RaptorQ Unit Tests (Encode/Decode, Chunking, DoS Bounds)","description":"# TEST: RaptorQ Unit Tests


## Goal
Prove RaptorQ encode/decode correctness and enforce decode DoS bounds (size/time) with reproducible test vectors.

## Scope
Comprehensive unit tests for RaptorQ symbol encoding/decoding and chunking.

## Test Categories

### Basic Encoding/Decoding
- [ ] Encode small object (< symbol size) → decode from K symbols
- [ ] Encode medium object (multiple symbols) → decode from K symbols
- [ ] Encode large object → ChunkedObjectManifest path
- [ ] Decode with exactly K source symbols
- [ ] Decode with K + repair symbols (erasure recovery)
- [ ] Decode with subset of repair symbols only

### Symbol Parameters
- [ ] Default symbol size (1024 bytes) produces expected symbol count
- [ ] Custom symbol sizes work correctly
- [ ] K (source symbols) calculation is correct for object sizes
- [ ] Repair ratio produces expected number of repair symbols

### ChunkedObjectManifest
- [ ] Objects > threshold produce manifest + raw chunks
- [ ] Manifest references correct chunk ObjectIds
- [ ] Chunks reconstruct to original object
- [ ] Chunk ordering is deterministic

### Decode DoS Mitigation
- [ ] Reject decode attempts exceeding CPU budget
- [ ] Reject decode attempts exceeding memory budget
- [ ] Timeout on pathological decode patterns
- [ ] Reject impossibly large symbol counts
- [ ] Reject impossibly large object sizes in manifest

### Symbol Identity
- [ ] Same object always produces same symbols (deterministic)
- [ ] ESI (encoding symbol identifier) is unique per symbol
- [ ] Symbols are self-describing (include ESI, K)

### Partial Availability
- [ ] DecodeStatus correctly reports received vs needed
- [ ] SymbolAck correctly acknowledges specific ESIs
- [ ] Missing symbol requests are targeted (not full retransmit)

## Golden Vectors
- 1KB object → expected symbols (with specific bytes)
- 100KB object → expected manifest structure
- Known erasure pattern → successful decode

## Fuzz Targets
- Malformed symbol streams
- Invalid ESI values
- Oversized object claims
- Corrupt manifest structures

## Performance Benchmarks
- Encode 1MB object: target < 10ms
- Decode 1MB from K symbols: target < 20ms
- Decode 1MB with 10% erasure: target < 50ms

## Logging Requirements
All tests emit:
```json
{
  "test_name": "...",
  "object_size": 12345,
  "symbol_size": 1024,
  "k": 13,
  "repair_symbols": 3,
  "decode_symbols_used": 13,
  "result": "pass|fail",
  "duration_us": 1234
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- All encoding/decoding paths tested
- DoS mitigations verified
- Performance benchmarks pass
- Golden vectors match

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-krt","title":"fcp-telemetry: Unified Metrics, Logging, and Tracing Library","description":"# fcp-telemetry: Unified Metrics, Logging, and Tracing Library

- Label/tag support

- Span creation and nesting
- Context propagation (W3C Trace Context)
- Trace ID generation
- Span attributes
- Sampling strategies
- Cross-connector correlation

## Export Formats
- Prometheus exposition format
- OTLP (OpenTelemetry Protocol)
- JSON logs to stdout
- Custom sinks

## Integration
- tracing crate integration
- HTTP middleware for auto-instrumentation
- FCP operation decoration
- Health endpoint exposure

## Performance
- Low overhead (<1% CPU)
- Async export
- Batch flushing
- Sampling support

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-kt9r","title":"[FCP2] Credential Injection: SecretObject + CredentialObject + Secretless Egress (Zeroize)","description":"# [FCP2] Credential Injection: SecretObject + CredentialObject + Secretless Egress (Zeroize)

## Goal
Define how credentials (API keys, OAuth tokens, webhook secrets, database passwords, client certs) flow to connectors in a way that is:
- mechanically capability-gated
- single-zone bound
- audited and explainable
- secret-safe (no disk; no logs; prefer no plaintext in connector memory)

## Why this is MVP
Connectors cannot do useful work without credentials, and credentials are one of the highest-value compromise targets. If we get this wrong, every connector becomes an exfiltration vector.

This bead defines the *platform contract* for credential access, so connector authors can implement auth once and rely on mechanical enforcement.

## NORMATIVE object model (Spec + `docs/fcp_model_connectors_rust.md`)

### 1) `SecretObject` + `SecretAccessToken`
- Secrets are stored/represented as mesh objects (`SecretObject`).
- Using/reconstructing a secret requires an explicit, short-lived authorization (`SecretAccessToken`).
- Every successful access MUST emit an `AuditEvent` (e.g., `event_type = "secret.access"`) and MUST NOT include secret bytes.
- Secret material MUST be zeroized immediately after use.

Note: Threshold secrets (k-of-n) and wrapped shares are part of the spec security model. Even if we ship a simplified MVP path, the *interfaces* must not preclude threshold reconstruction.

### 2) `CredentialObject` + `CredentialId` ("secretless" by default)
A `CredentialObject` is a zone-bound, auditable handle describing *how to apply* a secret to outbound traffic:
- maps `CredentialId` -> `SecretId`
- defines application method (e.g., HTTP header format)
- optionally binds allowed hosts for defense-in-depth

Connectors SHOULD NOT receive raw credential bytes. They should reference a `CredentialId` in egress requests and rely on MeshNode enforcement.

### 3) Capability constraints: `credential_allow`
Capabilities grant *permission to use credentials* by listing allowed `CredentialId`s:
- `CapabilityConstraints.credential_allow` is the authoritative allow-list.
- Credential permissions are checked at the boundary (egress proxy / host), not by connector convention.

## Preferred flow: secretless credential injection via MeshNode egress proxy
1. Connector prepares an `EgressHttpRequest` (or `EgressTcpConnectRequest`) and sets `credential = Some(CredentialId)`.
2. Egress proxy verifies ALL of the following:
   - `CredentialId` ∈ invoking capability’s `credential_allow`
   - destination host/port conforms to `NetworkConstraints`
   - destination host is allowed by the referenced `CredentialObject.host_allow` (when present)
   - policy allows the operation’s safety tier in this zone (fail closed)
3. Proxy fetches/validates the referenced `CredentialObject` and `SecretObject` and injects credential material at the network boundary.
4. Proxy emits an `AuditEvent` describing usage (credential id, host, purpose) without any secret bytes.

This yields "secretless connectors" where plaintext credentials never enter connector memory.

## When secret materialization is unavoidable (explicit, audited, bounded)
Some connectors may need to sign payloads locally (HMAC), perform mTLS client auth, or interact with non-proxied libraries.

In these cases:
- The connector (or host on its behalf) must obtain a `SecretAccessToken`.
- Reconstruction/use must be short-lived and bounded.
- Every materialization MUST be audited.
- Dangerous operations MUST require explicit ApprovalToken policy (and emit DecisionReceipt on deny).
- Secret bytes MUST be zeroized on all paths, including errors and panics.

## Manifest / schema integration requirements
- Credential IDs MUST NOT be encoded inside capability IDs.
- Operations that need credentials must require a capability whose constraints include the needed `CredentialId` in `credential_allow`.
- If credentials are host-bound, host canonicalization rules MUST match the NetworkConstraints canonicalization rules.

## Tests (owned by `flywheel_connectors-nnja`)
- Unit: `CredentialObject` validation (format, host binding, canonicalization)
- Unit: `credential_allow` enforcement logic (allow/deny)
- Integration: egress proxy injection (header injection, deny wrong host, deny missing `credential_allow`)
- Adversarial: prove connectors cannot obtain secret bytes unless explicitly granted and audited
- Audit: `secret.access` events emitted with redaction guarantees

## Acceptance Criteria
- Secretless credential injection works end-to-end with mechanical enforcement at the boundary.
- No secrets touch disk; no secret bytes appear in logs or error messages.
- Unauthorized credential use fails closed with a DecisionReceipt and evidence.
- Secret materialization (when required) is explicit, audited, short-lived, and zeroized.

After deserialization, tokens are CapabilityToken<Unverified> — they CANNOT have their claims accessed. Only after calling verify(key) which returns CapabilityToken<Verified> can claims be read. Functions that accept capability operations (invoke, simulate, etc.) require CapabilityToken<Verified>. WHY: A7 Type-Theoretic found that token verification is OPTIONAL in the current API. A programmer can deserialize a token and use its claims without ever calling verify(). H2 Adversarial found holder-binding bypass and clock-skew attacks that succeed when verification is skipped. The phantom type pattern makes it IMPOSSIBLE to use unverified claims — the compiler rejects it. IMPLEMENTATION SKETCH: ```rust struct Unverified; struct Verified; pub struct CapabilityToken<State = Unverified> { raw: CoseToken, _phantom: PhantomData<State>, } impl CapabilityToken<Unverified> { pub fn deserialize(bytes: &[u8]) -> Result<Self, Error> { ... } pub fn verify(self, key: &Ed25519VerifyingKey) -> Result<CapabilityToken<Verified>, Error> { // Consuming method — Unverified token is gone after this // Verify signature, expiry, revocation, holder binding Ok(CapabilityToken { raw: self.raw, _phantom: PhantomData }) } } impl CapabilityToken<Verified> { pub fn capability_id(&self) -> &str { ... } pub fn zone_id(&self) -> &ZoneId { ... } pub fn constraints(&self) -> &CapabilityConstraints { ... } } // Functions requiring verified tokens: async fn invoke(token: &CapabilityToken<Verified>, req: InvokeRequest) -> ... ``` WHERE: crates/fcp-core/src/capability.rs — CapabilityToken struct. crates/fcp-crypto/src/cose.rs — CoseToken. crates/fcp-sdk/src/lib.rs — FcpConnector trait (invoke signature). All callers that currently access token claims must be updated. BLAST RADIUS: This is a breaking change to the CapabilityToken API. All code that deserializes tokens must now call verify() before accessing claims. Estimate 30-50 call sites across platform crates and connectors. ACCEPTANCE: (1) CapabilityToken<Unverified> has NO methods exposing claims. (2) verify() is a consuming method returning CapabilityToken<Verified>. (3) All claim-accessing methods only on CapabilityToken<Verified>. (4) invoke/simulate/introspect signatures require Verified. (5) Compilation fails if any code path uses unverified claims. (6) At least 8 tests: deserialize-then-verify happy path, deserialize-without-verify compile error (doc test), expired token rejected by verify, revoked token rejected by verify, holder mismatch rejected, clock-skew tolerance works, serialization round-trip, verify-then-access-claims. ESTIMATED EFFORT: 3-4 days (type change + 30-50 call sites + tests). DESIGN NOTE: The consuming verify() method means the Unverified token is MOVED into the Verified token. The original cannot be reused. This prevents use-before-verify and double-verify patterns. The PhantomData<State> adds zero runtime cost — it is erased at compilation.","created_at":"2026-04-08T03:36:47Z"}]}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-oy5","title":"fcp.sentry: Alert rule configuration and management","description":"# fcp.sentry: Alert rule configuration and management (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide controlled access to Sentry alert rule configuration so agents can help maintain:
- issue alerts
- performance alerts
- notification routing

This is **dangerous** (high blast radius). Must be approval-gated by policy.

## Scope
### Read
- List alert rules (project/org scope where available).
- Get rule definition + current enabled/disabled state.

### Write (dangerous)
- Create/update/delete alert rules.
- Enable/disable rule.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `sentry.alerts.read`
- `sentry.alerts.write` (dangerous; requires approval)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- For any write:
  - require ApprovalToken when policy dictates (recommended default)
  - emit OperationReceipt + AuditEvent with:
    - rule id
    - structural hash of rule definition
    - correlation id

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `sentry.alert.list`
  - `sentry.alert.get`
  - `sentry.alert.create`
  - `sentry.alert.update`
  - `sentry.alert.enable`
  - `sentry.alert.disable`
  - `sentry.alert.delete`
- Safe rule templates:
  - avoid arbitrary code/script injection
  - prefer constrained rule DSL + parameterization

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - CRUD flows
  - validation failures
  - approval gating
  - audit/receipt emission

## Acceptance criteria
- Alert rule changes are mechanically gated, auditable, and reversible.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-oz1v","title":"TEST: Rate Limiting Unit Tests (ThrottleViolation, Backpressure, Quota Enforcement)","description":"# TEST: Rate Limiting Unit Tests


## Goal
Validate rate limiting / backpressure behaviors (quota, throttling, time-bounds) are correct and produce explainable denials.

## Scope
Comprehensive tests for rate limiting and quota enforcement.

## Test Categories

### ThrottleViolation Detection
- [ ] Requests exceeding rate produce ThrottleViolation
- [ ] Violation includes retry_after_ms
- [ ] Violation includes quota details
- [ ] Violation is logged to audit

### Backpressure Signals
- [ ] Backpressure signal sent when approaching limit
- [ ] Client respects backpressure hint
- [ ] Gradual backoff before hard rejection
- [ ] Backpressure clears when load drops

### Quota Enforcement
- [ ] Per-connector quotas enforced
- [ ] Per-zone quotas enforced
- [ ] Per-operation quotas enforced
- [ ] Quota reset on time window
- [ ] Quota pooling across operations

### Burst Handling
- [ ] Token bucket allows bursts
- [ ] Burst capacity is configurable
- [ ] Burst doesn't exceed hard limits
- [ ] Burst refill rate is correct

### Distributed Rate Limiting
- [ ] Quotas shared across mesh nodes
- [ ] Quota sync is eventually consistent
- [ ] No double-spending on quota
- [ ] Partition handling for quotas

## Golden Vectors
Location: `tests/vectors/rate_limiting/`
- `throttle_violation.cbor`
- `backpressure_signal.cbor`
- `quota_state.cbor`

## Logging Requirements
```json
{
  "timestamp": "RFC3339",
  "test_name": "test_quota_enforcement",
  "phase": "execute",
  "correlation_id": "uuid",
  "connector_id": "...",
  "quota_used": 95,
  "quota_limit": 100,
  "throttled": false,
  "result": "pass"
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- Rate limits are enforced correctly
- Quotas prevent resource exhaustion
- Audit trail for all throttle events

WHY: J1 Deontic found this is a DIRECT VIOLATION of the default-deny principle. A connector that does not override simulate() currently implicitly claims ALL operations are allowed. This means a new connector scaffold that ships without implementing simulate() silently tells the system it can handle anything. The correct default is: deny everything the connector has not explicitly declared it can simulate. WHERE: crates/fcp-core/src/connector.rs or crates/fcp-sdk/src/lib.rs — the default impl of simulate() in the FcpConnector trait. Also update fwc new_cmd.rs scaffold template to include a simulate() override with proper operation matching. ACCEPTANCE: (1) Default simulate() returns denied. (2) Existing connectors that rely on default must be updated (or explicitly override with allowed for their operations). (3) fwc new scaffold includes simulate() override template. (4) At least 3 tests: default returns denied, override returns allowed, denied includes reason code. ESTIMATED EFFORT: 1 day for the default change + 0.5 day per connector that needs updating. The correct default is: deny everything the connector has not explicitly declared it can simulate. WHERE: crates/fcp-core/src/connector.rs or crates/fcp-sdk/src/lib.rs — the default impl of simulate() in the FcpConnector trait.
